metadata.summary: Semantic token layer maps primitives to purpose-based aliases with different values per mode. Three tiers, every alias in every mode, contrast pairs co-located for automatable validation.

content.title: Semantic Tokens and Modes
content.summary: A semantic token layer maps primitive values (`color.blue.500`) to purpose-based aliases (`color.action.primary`) that resolve to different values per mode (light/dark, high-contrast, compact density, brand). Modes are declared per collection in Figma Variables or token JSON; Style Dictionary emits platform-specific output per mode. Every alias must have a value in every declared mode, and contrast pairs must be co-located so validation is automatable.

why.p: Hardcoding primitive tokens in components couples visual decisions to component code. A semantic layer decouples them: adding a dark-mode means adding one set of semantic alias resolutions, not touching every component. Aliasing depth must stay at three levels (primitive → semantic → component) to keep runtime debugging tractable and contrast validation automated.

list.item: Building a token system that supports light/dark, high-contrast, density, or brand modes.
Migrating from raw color/spacing tokens to a semantic layer.
Wiring Figma Variables to Style Dictionary so designers and engineers share one source of truth.
Adding a new mode without doubling component code paths.
Single-theme apps where modes add cost without payoff — start with raw tokens, promote when the second theme arrives.
Print or static asset pipelines where mode-switching does not apply.
Pure animation/motion tokens where mode semantics are rarely meaningful.

rule.statement: Use three tiers exactly — primitive, semantic, component — and ensure component tokens reference semantic tokens, never primitives.
rule.rationale: Component-to-primitive coupling means every primitive rename requires touching component definitions; the semantic layer is the decoupling boundary.

list.item: Primitive: `color.blue.500 = #3B82F6` — raw value, no design intent.
Semantic: `color.action.primary → {color.blue.500}` — purpose-based alias.
Component: `button.primary.bg → {color.action.primary}` — exception only, must have `$description`.

example.title: Semantic alias with mode values in Figma Variables format

rule.statement: Every alias must have a value in every declared mode; missing values cause silent fallbacks that ship wrong colors.
rule.rationale: A mode with a missing alias resolves to undefined behavior; different platforms handle undefined differently, making the regression unpredictable and hard to reproduce.

rule.statement: Reserve one mode per axis as the explicit default; document inheritance rules so consumers of unset modes fall back deterministically.
rule.rationale: Implicit inheritance from an undocumented default causes surprise regressions when the default mode is renamed or reordered.

rule.statement: Bound mode combinations by design decision, not by combinatorial exhaustion; mode explosion (4 color × 3 density × 5 brands = 60 configs) kills maintainability.
rule.rationale: Each supported configuration requires validation; unchecked mode growth creates an exponential testing burden that makes additions prohibitively expensive.

rule.statement: Co-locate contrast partner pairs in the token spec (e.g., `text.on-surface-primary` declares its surface partner); validation requires both halves.
rule.rationale: WCAG contrast must be verified as a pair, not per token; without co-location the validator cannot determine which text color applies to which surface color in each mode.

rule.statement: Generate runtime CSS custom properties at build time, not at app boot; boot-time JS theming causes flicker and hurts Core Web Vitals.
rule.rationale: CSS custom properties set at boot add a render-blocking JS execution step before first paint; build-time generation eliminates this entirely.

antipattern.description: Per-platform deltas (iOS 14px button padding) expressed as inline overrides on a shared token (`"ios": "14px"`)
antipattern.reason: Inline platform overrides are invisible to designers in Figma and to linters; use separate platform-prefixed tokens instead.

rule.statement: Use agents for mechanical token operations (alias generation, mode-matrix expansion, contrast checks, format conversion); keep humans on naming and semantic decisions.
rule.rationale: Agents are reliable at deterministic transformations but poor at naming intent; auto-promoted primitive-to-semantic names are the one place where human meaning is irreplaceable.

list.item: Step 1: `token-aliaser` (haiku) — deterministic mapping of primitive to semantic from a rules file.
Step 2: `mode-validator` (haiku) — verifies every (alias × mode) cell has a value and meets contrast requirements.
Step 3: `multi-platform-emitter` — runs Style Dictionary to write CSS, iOS, Android, JSON.
Trigger: re-run on every Figma library version bump in CI.

rule.statement: Treat a token rename as a breaking API change; use codemods or alias deprecation periods, never silent removals.
rule.rationale: Consumers reference tokens as strings in code, config, and MDX; a silent removal breaks builds at runtime without compile-time warning.

rule.statement: Run the mode coverage check in CI on every PR that touches token source files; block on any missing (alias, mode) pair.
rule.rationale: A missing value detected in CI costs a fix; a missing value that ships costs a themed-product regression report, a hotfix, and user trust.

antipattern.description: LLM invents token names from a closed enumeration without pinning to the manifest.
antipattern.reason: Pin the agent to a closed enumeration loaded from the project manifest; reject outputs with identifiers not present in the manifest.

antipattern.description: Color math regressions are silent: agent emits `oklch()` values that look fine but fail contrast in dark mode.
antipattern.reason: Always run the mode-validator post-generation; visual inspection is insufficient for contrast in wide-gamut color spaces.

antipattern.description: Figma Variables REST API uses `tempId` for newly-created variables; agent reuses a stale ID in a dependent operation.
antipattern.reason: Capture and reuse `tempId` values within the same API call batch; stale IDs create dangling references that are silent until the file is reloaded.

antipattern.description: Agent reorders Style Dictionary transforms while "cleaning up" the config.
antipattern.reason: Style Dictionary transforms run in declared order; reordering silently changes platform output. Lock the order in the agent's tooling spec.

antipattern.description: Agent auto-promotes primitives to semantic layer without human review.
antipattern.reason: Semantic naming is the one step that adds real human meaning; an auto-promoted name like `color.blue-500-alias` is worse than no semantic layer.

list.item: `token-aliaser` — haiku; deterministic mapping primitive→semantic from a rules file.
`mode-validator` — haiku; verifies every (alias × mode) cell has a value and passes contrast/spec checks.
`token-diff-reporter` — sonnet; summarizes additions/removals/breaking changes between two token snapshots for release notes.
`figma-syncer` — sonnet; handles Figma Variables REST mutations with idempotent payloads.
`style-dictionary` — Multi-platform token build (CSS, iOS, Android, JSON). Install: `npm i -g style-dictionary`. Docs: amzn.github.io/style-dictionary
`theo` (Salesforce) — Token transforms similar to Style Dictionary. Docs: github.com/salesforce-ux/theo
`tokens-studio` plugin + sync CLI — Figma Variables ↔ JSON sync. Website: tokens.studio
`figma-api-exporter` (community) — Pull Figma Variables via REST. Docs: github.com/figma/api-spec
`culori` — Programmatic color manipulation, contrast. Install: `npm i culori`. Website: culorijs.org
`axe-core` CLI — Contrast/a11y validation on rendered modes. Install: `npm i -g @axe-core/cli`
`chroma.js` — Color conversion, palettes. Website: gka.github.io/chroma.js
LLMs invent token names confidently. Pin the agent to a closed enumeration loaded from a manifest; reject outputs with unknown identifiers.
Color math regressions are silent: agent may emit `oklch()` values that look fine but fail contrast in dark mode. Always run `mode-validator` post-generation.
Figma Variables REST API uses `tempId` for newly-created variables in the same call. Agents must capture and reuse those IDs in dependent operations or risk dangling refs.
Mode counts are per-collection; agents that flatten across collections can lose mode info. Preserve collection scope end-to-end.
Avoid agent-driven "auto-promotion" of primitives to semantic without human review — semantic naming is the one place humans add real meaning.
Cache invalidation: when tokens change, downstream artifacts (Storybook stories, Tailwind config, iOS asset catalog) must rebuild. Drive with file hashes, not timestamps.
Style Dictionary transforms run in declared order; an agent reordering them silently can break platform output. Lock the order in the agent's tooling spec.
Mode explosion: 4 color modes × 3 density modes × 5 brands = 60 effective configurations. Methodology does not bound combinatorial growth.
Figma Variables have hard limits (collection size, mode count per plan); methodology does not surface those.
iOS/Android/Web have different runtime mode-switching semantics (system colorScheme vs CSS media query vs Compose `LocalConfiguration`); naming alone does not guarantee parity.
"Semantic" is subjective — `text.primary` means different things to different designers; without governance the layer rots.
High-contrast and AAA contrast modes are not free additions — they require ratio testing on every alias before claiming support.

template.purpose: Script that fails if any alias is missing a value in any declared mode.
Structured-output prompt for a mode-validator subagent including contrast checks.
